content.text: is the practice of treating ML experiments as immutable, versioned artifacts—by snapshotting the
codebase state, data version, hyperparameters, pipeline configuration, and resulting metrics
for each experiment run—so you can reproduce, compare, and restore any past experiment at any time, rather than just logging metrics in a dashboard.
Tracking experiments = logging metrics, parameters, and artifacts in a dashboard. Versioning experiments = codifying experiments as code so logs are
always connected to the exact data, code, and pipeline that produced them
for each experiment that captures everything needed to reproduce it.
“Experiment versioning treats experiments as code. It saves all metrics, hyperparameters, and artifact information in text files that can be versioned.”
You log metrics, parameters, Git commit, etc.
- Manually trace back to the exact code commit - Manually restore the exact data version - Manually reconstruct the pipeline
Tracking alone doesn’t guarantee you can
Connects experiment logs to exact data, code, and pipeline
: Run the same experiment again with the same setup -
: See how metrics changed across iterations -

“Versioned reproducibility means tracking changes to the experiment state. DVC can determine what changes were introduced by the experiment and only run what’s necessary.”
What gets versioned for each experiment
Git commit hash of the training/evaluation code
Data version (hash, DVC version, dataset ID)
linking results to the exact code and data.
Experiment versioning with DVC (Data Version Control)
DVC is the most popular tool for
Initialize DVC in a Git repo
This creates a structured project with: -
`` DVC: - Caches pipeline outputs - Versioned metrics, parameters, and artifacts - Links them to the Git commit
`` This restores: - Exact code version - Exact data version - Exact parameters and metrics Without needing to manually reconstruct anything.
- DVC can show which experiments changed, what changed, and only re-run what’s necessary

Associating each experiment run with the

procedure version_experiment(experiment_name, commit_message, repo_path, create_branch, stage_all):
  1. Locate Git repo
  2. Stage changes (if any)
  3. Create commit with experiment name
  4. Create branch: ragas/{experiment_name}
  5. Return commit hash as version identifier
(commit hash) linked to the experiment name.
Code, data, pipeline, parameters, metrics, artifacts
Traceability of model evolution, backtracking changes

of experiment versioning: each model version typically comes from one (or more) experiment versions.
1. Use a meaningful version naming system

Reduce manual effort and risk of oversight

- Source code - Configuration - Upstream requirements (datasets) - Generated output
- All in a
any past experiment can be restored to its exact state — same code commit, data pointer, hyperparameters, and environment — and re-run to produce identical results.
each metric is provably linked to the inputs and code that produced it, making it possible to explain and defend model behaviour to stakeholders or regulators.
teams can try bold changes knowing any previous version can be restored. This removes the psychological cost of experimentation.
a searchable experiment history means insights are not lost when team members leave. Future engineers can query which combinations were tried and why they were abandoned.
when a model degrades after a data update, bisecting the experiment history (analogous to git bisect) pinpoints which change caused the regression.

versioning model checkpoints with DVC or W&B Artifacts can consume hundreds of gigabytes of remote storage. Mitigate by setting retention policies — keep the top-N checkpoints by metric rather than every run.

integrating DVC into an existing training pipeline requires restructuring the project (params.yaml, dvc.yaml, remote storage config). This upfront investment pays off quickly on large projects but may be disproportionate for one-off experiments.

versioning only works if every team member actually commits params and pushes data. One person running experiments outside the system breaks the chain of provenance. Enforce through CI checks or automated training pipelines.
migrating from one experiment tracking tool (e.g., MLflow to W&B) requires re-importing historical runs. Choose tooling aligned with team size and infrastructure early, as migration is costly.
a model will go to production. Reproducing the exact production model for debugging or retraining is non-negotiable once real users depend on it.
running more than a handful of experiments per week. Without versioning, experiment results recorded manually in spreadsheets drift out of sync with the actual code and data.
multiple team members run experiments on the same codebase. Experiment versioning is the shared ledger that prevents conflicts and duplication.

you are in early exploratory research and the primary goal is rapid iteration, not reproducibility. A notebook with git-committed snapshots is sufficient until the project matures into a training pipeline.

attrs.alt: Experiment versioning: code, data, hyperparams, pipeline, environment -> atomic run -> metrics, artifacts, comparison history

content.text: The concrete implementation uses DVC layered on Git. DVC stores data and model files in remote storage (S3, GCS, or local disk) and commits lightweight pointer files (.dvc) to Git. Parameters and metrics are plain YAML/JSON files committed alongside the code, so every experiment corresponds to a Git ref that captures the complete state — code, data pointer, hyperparameters, pipeline definition, and resulting metrics.

# One-time setup
git init experiment-repo && cd experiment-repo
pip install dvc
dvc init
git add . && git commit -m "chore: init DVC"

# Track data with DVC
dvc add data/train.csv data/val.csv
git add data/train.csv.dvc .gitignore
git commit -m "data: add training set v1"
dvc push   # upload to remote

# Create experiment structure
dvc exp init python train.py \
  --params params.yaml \
  --metrics metrics.json

cat > params.yaml << 'EOF'
model:
  architecture: resnet50
  dropout: 0.3
train:
  lr: 0.001
  epochs: 20
  batch_size: 32
EOF
git add params.yaml dvc.yaml && git commit -m "exp: baseline config"

# Run experiments
dvc exp run --name baseline
dvc exp run --set-param train.lr=0.01 --name lr-1e-2
dvc exp run --set-param model.dropout=0.5 --name dropout-0.5

# Compare
dvc exp show --md
dvc metrics diff baseline lr-1e-2

# Reproduce any past experiment exactly
dvc exp restore lr-1e-2
dvc repro
Training Script: Reading from params.yaml, Writing to metrics.json

# train.py — DVC tracks params.yaml and metrics.json automatically
import json, math, yaml
from pathlib import Path

params = yaml.safe_load(Path("params.yaml").read_text())
lr      = params["train"]["lr"]
epochs  = params["train"]["epochs"]
dropout = params["model"]["dropout"]

print(f"Training | lr={lr} | dropout={dropout} | epochs={epochs}")

best_acc = 0.0
best_loss = float("inf")

for epoch in range(1, epochs + 1):
    # Replace with real training loop
    val_loss = 2.0 * math.exp(-0.15 * epoch * lr * 1000) + 0.05 * (1 + dropout)
    val_acc  = 1.0 - val_loss / 2.5
    best_acc  = max(best_acc, val_acc)
    best_loss = min(best_loss, val_loss)
    if epoch % 5 == 0:
        print(f"  epoch {epoch}: val_loss={val_loss:.4f}, val_acc={val_acc:.4f}")

# DVC reads this file after the run
metrics = {"accuracy": round(best_acc, 4), "val_loss": round(best_loss, 4)}
Path("metrics.json").write_text(json.dumps(metrics, indent=2))
print(f"Metrics: {metrics}")

import mlflow
import subprocess

def git_sha() -> str:
    return subprocess.run(["git", "rev-parse", "HEAD"],
                         capture_output=True, text=True).stdout.strip()[:12]

mlflow.set_experiment("resnet-sweep")

for lr, dropout in [(0.001, 0.3), (0.01, 0.3), (0.001, 0.5)]:
    with mlflow.start_run(run_name=f"lr{lr}_do{dropout}"):
        mlflow.set_tag("git_commit", git_sha())      # code version
        mlflow.set_tag("data_version", "train-v1")   # data version
        mlflow.log_params({"lr": lr, "dropout": dropout})

        # Simulated metrics (replace with real training)
        acc  = 0.80 + lr * 5 - dropout * 0.05
        loss = 0.50 - lr * 2 + dropout * 0.10
        mlflow.log_metrics({"accuracy": acc, "val_loss": loss})
        print(f"  lr={lr}, dropout={dropout}: acc={acc:.4f}, loss={loss:.4f}")

print("\nView runs: mlflow ui")
# runs_df = mlflow.search_runs()  # DataFrame for programmatic comparison
Real-World Example: LLM Fine-tuning Experiment Registry

Fine-tuning large language models is expensive — a single run can cost hundreds of dollars in GPU time. Without experiment versioning, teams cannot trace which LoRA rank, learning rate schedule, and dataset version produced a given evaluation score. The pattern below versions each fine-tuning configuration as a Git commit, making every run traceable and reproducible from the same Git repository that holds the training code.

from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from pathlib import Path
import json, subprocess, yaml

@dataclass
class LLMFineTuneConfig:
    base_model: str = "meta-llama/Llama-3-8B"
    lora_r: int = 16
    lora_alpha: int = 32
    lora_dropout: float = 0.05
    lr: float = 2e-4
    epochs: int = 3
    batch_size: int = 8
    dataset_version: str = "customer-support-v3.2"

def version_run(config: LLMFineTuneConfig, name: str) -> str:
    """Commit config to params.yaml, return Git commit hash as run ID."""
    Path("params.yaml").write_text(yaml.dump(asdict(config)))
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", "params.yaml"], check=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "commit", "-m",
                    f"exp: {name} lora_r={config.lora_r} lr={config.lr}"],
                   check=True)
    sha = subprocess.run(["git", "rev-parse", "HEAD"],
                         capture_output=True, text=True).stdout.strip()[:12]
    print(f"Run versioned: {name} @ {sha}")
    return sha

def record_metrics(sha: str, metrics: dict):
    Path("metrics.json").write_text(
        json.dumps({"run_id": sha, **metrics}, indent=2))

# Usage:
# sha = version_run(LLMFineTuneConfig(), "baseline")
# ... run training ...
# record_metrics(sha, {"helpfulness": 0.82, "accuracy": 0.78})
# To reproduce: git checkout <sha> && dvc repro

# Demonstrate config comparison without running training:
baseline   = LLMFineTuneConfig()
rank32_run = LLMFineTuneConfig(lora_r=32, lora_alpha=64)
print("Baseline config:", {k: v for k, v in asdict(baseline).items() if k in ('lora_r','lr','dataset_version')})
print("Rank-32 config: ", {k: v for k, v in asdict(rank32_run).items() if k in ('lora_r','lr','dataset_version')})
Logging metrics without linking to code version.
A dashboard run with no git_commit tag is unreproducible. Always log the Git SHA and data version alongside every metric.
Versioning only the model checkpoint, not the training code.
A model.pkl without the code that produced it cannot be safely retrained or debugged.
Running the same code on a silently updated dataset produces different results. Always version data with DVC and reference the version in every experiment record.
Storing hyperparameters only in the tracking dashboard.
Dashboards are queryable but not self-contained. Store params in params.yaml committed to Git so the full state is in the repository.
PyTorch 2.0 and 2.1 can produce different numerics. Log requirements.txt or a conda environment file with every run.

DVC overhead is primarily storage. For 20 experiment runs with 8GB model checkpoints, total remote storage is ~160GB — roughly $3/month on S3. Git itself stays fast because it only stores lightweight .dvc pointer files, not the large binary artifacts. The dvc exp run wrapper adds a few seconds of hashing and caching overhead, imperceptible against hours-long training runs.

At large scale (hundreds of parallel experiments with distributed training), MLflow or Weights & Biases scale better than DVC because they use dedicated tracking servers with database backends. DVC uses Git stashes, which do not handle concurrent writes well. For teams running > 20 parallel experiments, use DVC for data versioning and MLflow/W&B for tracking, and link them by logging the DVC data version tag in the MLflow run.

The main reliability risk is remote storage failure: if the DVC remote or MLflow artifact store is unavailable, runs cannot be reproduced. Mitigate with S3 versioning, cross-region replication, and by committing checksums to Git even when artifacts are in remote storage. Never store credentials in params.yaml — use environment variables and reference them as ${env:MY_KEY}.
treats experiments as code by snapshotting code, data, pipeline, parameters, and metrics so you can reproduce and restore any experiment with a single command.
to the exact data, code, and pipeline that produced them.
is the main tool for experiment versioning, enabling:

to reproduce past experiments
- For LLM evaluation, experiment versioning creates

linked to commit hashes as reproducibility keys.
- Best practices: use meaningful version naming, automate logging, and treat each experiment as an atomic unit capturing code, data, config, and output.
